Your Code: abc = mean(mouse AUC, control) − mean(mouse AUC, treatment)
Xeva: abc = AUC(control mean-curve) − AUC(treatment mean-curve); replicates imputed onto a shared grid
Key Difference: Mathematically equivalent when mice share a measurement grid, since trapz is linear. ~92% of your batches don't align day-for-day, but control/treatment cadence is statistically identical — no systematic bias found.

Your Code: angle = mean(mouse angle, control) − mean(mouse angle, treatment)
Key Difference: Same reasoning as abc — regression-through-origin slope is linear in the volume values, so mean-of-slopes ≈ slope-of-mean-curve on a shared grid.

Xeva: (VC_last − VT_last) / (VC0 − VT0), from the two mean-curves
Key Difference: Deliberately different: Xeva's denominator (starting-volume gap between arms) is unstable when arms are size-matched, as yours are. Aggregation also differs — median-of-ratios vs. a single ratio — since ratios don't average linearly.
